title: Nowak v Data Protection Commission - 29 September 2025
text:
APPROVED 
NO REDACTION REQUIRED 

THE COURT OF APPEAL 
 
Record Number: 2024/262 
 
 Neutral Citation Number [2025] IECA 195 
Faherty J. 
Allen J. 
Meenan J 

BETWEEN/ 
 
PETER NOWAK 
 
APPELLANT 
 
- AND - 
 
THE DATA PROTECTION COMMISSIONER 
 
RESPONDENT 

JUDGMENT of Ms. Justice Faherty delivered on the 29th day of September 2025 

1. This is an appeal from the judgment of the High Court (Bradley J. hereafter “the 
Judge”) delivered on 2 July 2024 ([2024] IEHC 428) and consequent order made on 8 
October 2024 dismissing the appellant’s appeal against the judgment of the Circuit Court 
dismissing his appeal under s. 26(1)(d) of the Data Protection Acts 1988 – 2003 (hereafter 
for ease of reference “the 1988 Act” or “the Data Protection Acts”) against the decision of 
the Data Protection Commission (hereafter “the DPC” or “the Commissioner”) dated 21 
April 2022. 
Overview 

- 2 - 
2. The appeal is rather tangled. Before unravelling it, it is useful to take a step back to 
the event which gave rise to the appellant’s engagement with the DPC. 
3. In 2009 the appellant sat a professional examination set by the Institute of Chartered 
Accountants in Ireland: which styles itself Chartered Accountants Ireland (“CAI”). He was 
judged to have failed: and following an appeal to the CAI Appeals Panel, that result was 
confirmed. 
4. In 2010 the appellant submitted a personal data access request to CAI for release of 
all personal data held by CAI, and specifically for a copy of his examination scripts. He 
was provided with a bundle of copy documents and correspondence. CAI declined to 
provide the appellant with a copy of his examination scripts on the ground -it contended- 
that these were not personal data. 
5. The appellant filed a complaint with the DPC which culminated in a judgment of the 
Court of Justice of the European Union in 2017 which found that the appellant’s 
examination scripts were personal data: and in 2018 he was provided with copies and 
offered an opportunity to inspect the originals. 
6. In the meantime, the appellant had made other complaints to the DPC which were the 
subject of protracted litigation. By the time of the reference to the CJEU, the focus of 
attention on the appellant’s complaint was on the examination scripts and the DPC took the 
view that it had been finally disposed of by the provision of the copy scripts to the 
appellant. The appellant, however, did not share this view and in 2020 pressed the DPC to 
address a number of issues which he contended were outstanding. 
7. The foundation of the appellant’s appeal to this Court is his insistence that his 2010 
complaint, as reformulated or explained in 2020, was not adequately addressed. 
Background 

- 3 - 
8. On 1 July 2010, the appellant made a complaint to the DPC under the 1988 Act, 
which complaint was restated on 14 July 2010. The complaint concerned issues arising 
from the appellant’s data subject access request to CAI on or around 12 May 2010 seeking 
access to personal data of the appellant held by CAI arising from his enrolment and 
participation in a training scheme operated by CAI. In particular, he sought access to a 
copy of his examination scripts relating to his (then) recent “CAP2” examination and 
personal data concerning his appeal to the appeals panel relating to his failure of that 
examination. 
9. On 1 June 2010, CAI responded to that request, enclosing copies of seventeen 
documents. However, CAI declined to release the appellant’s examination scripts as it 
took the view, based on legal advice, that the examination scripts were not personal data. 
10. In the complaint the appellant filed with the DPC on 1 July 2010 he objected to the 
refusal to provide access to the examination scripts and expressed concerns about the 
“legitimacy” of certain documentation which had been released to him in response to his 
subject access request. As deposed to in the affidavit of Ms. Sandra Skehan of the DPC, 
upon an initial examination of the complaint the DPC agreed with CAI’s view that the 
examination scripts did not contain or comprise the appellant’s personal data and was of 
the view that the issues around the “legitimacy” of certain documentation furnished by 
CAI did not fall within the remit of the DPC. On that basis, the Office of the DPC 
declined to investigate the complaint on the merits. This was set out in correspondence sent 
by DPC to the appellant on 21 July 2010. In short, the appellant was advised that the 
Commissioner “has examined all the papers on this matter and has not identified any 
matter arising for investigation under the Data Protection Acts. The material over which 
you are seeking to exercise a right of correction is not personal data to which s. 6 of the 
Data Protection Acts applies. In accordance with s. 10(1)(b)(i) of the Data Protection 

- 4 - 
Acts, we are not obliged to investigate a complaint where no breach of the Acts can be 
identified.” As the DPC took the view that the complaint was not sustainable it was 
considered by the DPC to be in a technical sense “frivolous and vexatious” and that the 
Office was not obliged to further investigate it. 
11. The appellant contested this position in the courts. On his appeal under s. 26(1)(d) of 
the 1988 Act, the Circuit Court accepted the DPC’s argument that no appeal lay from a 
complaint that had been found to be frivolous and vexatious and so determined that it did 
not have jurisdiction to hear the appeal. On appeal, the High Court upheld that decision 
([2012] IEHC 449). The appellant further appealed to the Court of Appeal which upheld 
the decision of the High Court in an ex tempore judgment and by order dated 24 April 
2015. The appellant sought and was granted leave to appeal to the Supreme Court. In 
Nowak v. The Data Protection Commissioner [2016] IESC 585 the Supreme Court held 
that the fact that the DPC determined that a complaint was ill-founded did not preclude the 
appellant from bringing a statutory appeal pursuant to s. 26 of the 1988 Act. 
12. On a preliminary reference from the Supreme Court, in Case C-434/16, Nowak v. 
The Data Protection Commissioner EU:C:2017:994, the CJEU held that Article 2(a) of 
Directive 95/46/EC of 24 October 1995 “On the protection of individuals with regard to 
the processing of personal data and on the free movement of such data” “must be 
interpreted as meaning that in circumstances such as those in the main proceedings, the 
written answers submitted by a candidate at a professional examination and any comments 
made by an examiner with respect to those answers constitute personal data, within the 
meaning of that provision”. (para. 62) 
13. Following the CJEU’s ruling, on 13 March 2018, the Supreme Court made an Order 
on consent quashing the DPC’s decision not to investigate the appellant’s 2010 complaint 
and remitting the matter to the DPC “for investigation of the Appellant’s complaint”. 

- 5 - 
14. On 30 May 2018, CAI wrote to the appellant offering him copies of his examination 
scripts. Under cover of letter dated 18 June 2018, CAI’s solicitors furnished the appellant 
with copies of his scripts and receipt of same was confirmed by the appellant. By letter of 
11 July 2018, CAI offered to facilitate an inspection by the appellant of the original scripts 
under controlled conditions. The appellant declined to take up that offer. 
15. It is common case, that in tandem with his appeals from the DPC’s May 2010 
decision, the appellant chose to pursue separate litigation to try to establish a right, under 
data protection law, to the original scripts. 
16. Following correspondence received from CAI on 19 November 2013 to the effect 
that it had retained “copies” of the appellant’s examination data in response to the 
appellant’s earlier request for confirmation that CAI continued to hold the “originals” of 
his personal data, on 13 January 2013, the appellant lodged a further complaint with the 
DPC which stated inter alia that from CAI’s letter he had inferred that “the originals of the 
data were destroyed”. The DPC wrote to the appellant in relation to this complaint on 27 
January 2014 wherein it reiterated its contention that examination scripts did not consist of 
personal data within the meaning of the Data Protection Acts and stating that the right of 
access under s. 4 was a right to request a copy of personal data held by a data controller. 
The letter concluded that as the DPC had not identified grounds for investigation, a formal 
investigation would not be commenced. 
17. The appellant’s appeal in respect of this issue was dismissed by the Circuit Court 
(Deery J.) on 3 June 2014 from which the appellant then brought an appeal to the High 
Court on a point of law raising, inter alia, whether the Circuit Court judge was correct to 
determine that the appellant’s exam scripts did not constitute personal data and whether a 
data subject was entitled to access the originals of his or her personal data pursuant to s. 4 
of the data Protection Acts. 

- 6 - 
18. Prior to that appeal coming on for hearing in the High Court, the first by those issues 
had been determined in the appellant’s favour by the CJEU’s ruling, in 2017, in Case C-
434/16, Nowak v. The Data Protection Commissioner on foot of the reference made by the 
Supreme Court in respect of the appellant’s 2010 complaint. Ultimately, the only issue that 
fell to the High Court to determine in the context of the appellant’s 2014 complaint was 
whether the appellant was entitled to access or obtain the originals of his examination 
scripts. The High Court (Coffey J.) found against the appellant on that issue from which 
the appellant duly appealed, and which culminated in a written judgment of this Court on 1 
July 2020 in Nowak v Data Protection Commissioner [2020] IECA 174. 
19. Writing for the Court, Haughton J. dismissed the appellant’s appeal, holding that the 
obligation on CAI to provide the appellant with personal data did not include an obligation 
to provide the data in its original form or, in the case of a document, to provide the original 
of that document. The appellant’s application for leave to appeal that judgment was refused 
by the Supreme Court ([2020] IESCDET 144). 
20. Separate to the foregoing, it is common case that the appellant pursued a number of 
other issues against a range of parties, by means of litigation and by the filing of regulatory 
complaints, which issues can fairly be said to have their origin in his failure to pass his 
CAP2 examination. In relation to some of those issues, parallel complaints were submitted 
by the appellant to the DPC, including a fresh complaint against CAI made on 26 July 
2017, as well as separate complaints against the Courts Service (made on 14 August 2017) 
and the Irish Auditing and Accounting Supervisory Authority (made on 21 August 2017). 
21. At the point of release of the appellant’s examination scripts in June 2018, the 
appellant was continuing to pursue each of the 2017 complaints and the appellate process 
in relation to his 2014 complaint as referred to above. 

- 7 - 
22. In February and April 2019, a series of applications for judicial review were made by 
the appellant in which the High Court was asked to make orders compelling the DPC to 
deliver decisions in respect of his complaints. In the event, each of the applications for 
judicial review were struck out with no order on 3 December 2019, without a hearing on 
the merits, decisions having been delivered by the Commissioner in the intervening period 
in connection with the 2017 complaints. 
23. The appellant, however, continued to agitate his 2010 complaint against the CAI. 
Against the backdrop of threatened further litigation, and in light of certain submissions 
made by the appellant at a hearing before this Court on 5 May 2020 in the context of his 
appeal against the order of Coffey J., the DPC’s solicitors, by email of 7 May 2020, called 
on the appellant to clarify what issues he considered to be outstanding from his 2010 
complaint. In his email response to this correspondence on 12 May 2020, the appellant 
identified five issues which he claimed were outstanding. The five issues were expressed 
as follows: 
“I already communicated what should have been investigated by the DPC. 
Outstanding issues still to be investigated by the DPC are as such: 
a. Whether examination answers data existed in law in the context of 
the 1988 Act in light of the ICAI (Charter Amendment) Act 1966 (a 
new point that arose since the complaint was lodged); 
b. whether all personal data was provided in response to the access 
request of 12 May 2010, such as confirmation by the Appeals 
Executive that the appeal in [sic.] ineligible, an Appeal Panel’s 
report or any other record proving the consideration of my appeal 

- 8 - 
by it, the marking scheme supporting the marks awarded by the 
Examiner and Moderator…; 
c. whether the copies of the marking schemes and the reports of [sic.] 
Examiner and Moderator were legitimate; 
d. whether the ICAI implemented all necessary and required by law 
security measures and controls preventing the unauthorised 
amendments to the Examination marks data (recording and storing 
such data in pencil in my case is rather unacceptable and 
susceptible to fraud); 
e. whether the ICAI was correct to refuse access to originals of 
personal data to include the marking schemes”. 
24. In light of that correspondence, the DPC issued its decision of 21 April 2022. Prior 
to that the DPC had afforded the appellant an opportunity to make submissions on a draft 
decision. The appellant had declined that offer. Whilst counsel for the DPC sought to rely 
on this fact, nothing turns on it, in my view. The fact that the appellant may have declined 
to make submissions on the draft decision did not affect his entitlement to pursue the 
appellate processes provided for in the Data Protection Acts. 
The 21 April 2022 decision 
25. In its decision of 21 April 2022, the DPC concluded that none of the five points 
identified by the appellant as outstanding arising from his 2010 complaint could be 
sustained. 
26. In relation to the first issue raised by the appellant in his 12 May 2020 email, the 
DPC understood this to comprise an allegation that CAI had failed to comply with its 
obligations under the Institute of Chartered Accountants in Ireland (Charter Amendment) 

- 9 - 
Act, 1966, (No. 2 (Private) of 1966) as amended (“the 1966 Act”) in that it neglected to 
obtain certain required forms of approval for such of its examinations, appeals and training 
regulations which were operative between 2006 and 2009 with the result that CAI was 
acting unlawfully when it conducted the examinations in question the results of which 
were also alleged by the appellant to be “unlawful, invalid, of no legal effect and non-
existing in law”. The decision noted the appellant’s acknowledgement that this was a new 
point which had arisen after the lodging of the original 2010 complaint, a fact which, of 
itself, the DPC considered would justify the rejection of the complaint. However, without 
prejudice to that preliminary view, the DPC considered the substance of the first issue and 
was of the view that the point failed in circumstances where the very same point had been 
rejected by the DPC in a decision of 3 December 2019 in response to a separate complaint 
brought by the appellant against CAI on 26 July 2017. 
27. The 3 December 2019 decision had, in turn, been the subject of a statutory appeal 
brought by the appellant to the Circuit Court which appeal was dismissed by order of the 
Circuit Court (His Honour Judge O’Connor) on 23 November 2021. The DPC noted that 
no further appeal from that order was extant. In all of those circumstances, the DPC was 
satisfied that it could dispose of the first point on the basis of its prior decision of 3 
December 2019 which was to the effect that the DPC had no authority or jurisdiction to 
make any finding in relation to any alleged failure on the part of CAI to comply with its 
obligations under the 1966 Act. Accordingly, the allegations made by the appellant against 
CAI were “not sustainable and so must be dismissed”. The DPC further noted that in 
correspondence with the DPC in March 2018, the appellant himself had acknowledged that 
CAI’s compliance or otherwise with its obligations under the 1966 legislation was a matter 
outside the jurisdiction of the DPC. 

- 10 - 
28. In relation to the second issue alleged to be outstanding from 2010, viz, whether all 
the appellant’s personal data had been provided in response to his access request of 12 
May 2010, the DPC concluded, having reviewed the documentation with which the 
appellant was provided on 1 June 2010 and 18 June 2018, that the documents released to 
him on those dates contained the full extent of the personal data held by CAI arising from 
or relating to the subject access request. 
29. The DPC addressed the third issue raised, namely whether the copies of the marking 
schemes and the reports of the examiner and moderator were legitimate, by stating that 
issues in relation to the legitimacy of CAI’s schemes and reports did not fall within the 
DPC’s statutory remit, the scope of which was set (as far as the appellant’s complaint was 
concerned) primarily by reference to the Data Protection Acts. 
30. Insofar as the appellant had expressed concerns that the documents the subject of the 
appellant’s complaint may have been “the subject of some sort of manipulation”, the DPC 
had regard to the judgment of this Court in Nowak v Data Protection Commissioner [2020] 
IECA 174 (which concerned the appellant’s 2014 complaint against CAI) where Haughton 
J. was satisfied, as regards similar assertions of manipulation and “doctoring” of 
documents, that “the appellant cannot pursue the ‘doctoring’ argument as it is based on 
hypothetical facts. The DPC, and the Circuit Court on appeal, can only determine the 
complaint on the facts presented to them”. 
31. Accordingly, in the present case, the DPC concluded that “it is not a matter for the 
DPC to engage with or seek to determine entirely speculative allegations, based on 
hypothetical facts to the effect that one or more documents containing [the appellant’s] 
personal data may have been doctored. No evidence of any such doctoring has been put 
forward by [the appellant]”. Furthermore, the DPC noted that CAI had provided the 
appellant with an opportunity to inspect the original versions of the key documents to 

- 11 - 
which his allegations related, an opportunity which the appellant declined to avail of. For 
the reasons it set out, the DPC was satisfied that the third issue raised by the appellant 
could not be sustained. 
32. In relation to the fourth issue, namely whether CAI had implemented “all necessary 
and required by law security measures and controls preventing the unauthorised 
amendments to the examination marks data”, the DPC was of the view that for the same 
reasons as outlined in respect of the appellant’s third issue, it could not be required to 
investigate “speculative allegations of fraud having their origin in hypothetical facts, and 
relating, not to any discernible data protection issue, but rather to the integrity of the 
CAI’s exam procedures. Such an investigation is simply not within the statutory remit of 
this office”. Accordingly, it found the fourth issue not sustainable. 
33. In relation to the fifth issue, viz, whether CAI was correct to refuse access to 
originals of personal data, the DPC noted that this issue was addressed in Nowak v Data 
Protection Commissioner [2020] IECA 174 where Haughton J. held, on the facts before 
the Court, that the appellant was not entitled to access the originals of any examination 
scripts or related documents containing his personal data. The DPC was of the view that 
precisely the same held true in respect of the marking schemes relevant to the examination, 
save that, in the DPC’s view, an additional point also arose in that there was nothing before 
the DPC to suggest that the marking scheme contained any personal data relating to an 
identified exam candidate in any event. For those reasons the DPC found the fifth issue 
raised by the appellant in his May 2020 email also unsustainable. 
34. In summary, the DPC found none of the five matters which the appellant alleged 
remained to be dealt with arising from his 2010 complaint could be sustained and so the 
complaint as advanced by the appellant by reference to those five points was dismissed. 
The appeal to the Circuit Court 

- 12 - 
35. As provided for by s. 26 of the 1988 Act, the appellant appealed to the Circuit Court 
against the DPC’s decision. It is well established that the appeal provided for is not on the 
merits but rather an appeal against error. In line with the principles set out in Orange 
Limited v. Director of Telecoms (No. 2) [2000] 4 I.R. 159 and Nowak v The Data 
Protection Commissioner [2016] 2 I.R. 585, the burden was on the appellant to establish 
on the balance of probabilities that, taking the adjudicative process as a whole and paying 
appropriate deference to the expertise of the decision maker, the decision under appeal was 
vitiated by a serious or significant error or a series of such errors such as would justify 
setting aside the decision. 
36. In a judgment delivered on 9 October 2023 the Circuit Court (His Honour Judge 
O’Connor) was satisfied that the decision was not vitiated by a serious and significant error 
or series of such errors which justified setting aside the decision. The Circuit Court judge 
was satisfied that the DPC in its decision of 21 April 2022 had correctly set out the 
application of the law and the facts. He considered the appellant’s arguments “at best a 
subjective analysis of hypothetical facts of which he believes to be the issues of law and 
facts applicable”. In the view of the Circuit Court judge, this was done “without 
demonstrating any error of law or significant error of fact which would justify this court 
setting aside the decision under appeal”. The Circuit Court also found no basis for stating 
a case to the Court of Appeal. 
The appeal to the High Court 
37. By s. 26(3)(b) of the 1988 Act, an appeal lies to the High Court on a point of law 
against the judgment and order of the Circuit Court. In his notice of appeal, the appellant 
asserted the following points of law: - 
“1. The learned Circuit Court judge erred in law in upholding the Commissioner’s 
decision who failed to determine that the Institute of Chartered Accountants in 

- 13 - 
Ireland breached the Data Protection Acts 1988 & 2003 by releasing a copy of the 
examination exam script outside of the statutory time-frame; 
2. Did the Data Protection Commission have jurisdiction to rule on the legal status 
of the Institute of Chartered Accountants in Ireland’s [‘Institute’s’]Examinations 
Regulations or make a factual finding in this respect in the course of the 
investigation of the complaint? 
3. If the Institute’s Examinations Regulations concerning examinations conducted 
between 2006 -2009 were unlawful, invalid and of no legal effect, did the invalid 
regulations caused [recte. cause], in turn, the processing of the examinations and 
training contact data unlawful in the context of the Data Protection Acts 1988 & 
2003 and 95/46/EC Directive? 
4. Whether a mere assessment of a letter of complaint and supporting 
documentation by the Commissioner amounted to an investigation of the complaint 
pursuant to Section 10 of the Data Protection Acts 1988 & 2003; 
5. Whether the examinations answers (examinations scripts) data could be regarded 
as personal data in light of disallowed Examinations Regulations pursuant to Section 
6 of the Institute of Chartered Accountants in Ireland (Charter Amendment) Act 
1966; 
6. Further or other point of law advanced at the hearing of the appeal”. 
The High Court judgment 
38. At para. 19 of his judgment, the Judge helpfully set out the principles, as developed 
in the case law, applicable to an appeal to the High Court on a point of law, noting (citing 
on the decision of this Court in Fox v. The Data Protection Commissioner [2024] IECA 
92) that in an appeal point of law, the High Court is “significantly more confined than an 
appeal to the Circuit Court”. He referred to Nowak v Data Protection Commissioner 

- 14 - 
[2022] IECA 95 where this Court cited with approval the dictum of Clarke J. (as he then 
was) in Fitzgibbon v. The Law Society of Ireland [2015] 1 I.R. 516 that in appeals on a 
point of law, a higher degree of deference, so far as the facts are concerned, is paid by the 
appellate body to the decision made by the body of first instance, as opposed to the case in 
an appeal against error. He noted also the judgment of the Supreme Court in Attorney 
General v. Davis [2018] 2 I.R. 357 where McKechnie J. outlined the circumstances 
whereby a court may intervene to overturn a decision on a point of law, summarised by the 
Judge as follows: - 
(a) In cases of errors of law as generally understood, to include those mentioned in 
Fitzgibbon; 
(b) In cases involving errors such as would give rise to judicial review, including 
illegality, irrationality, defective or absence of reasoning and procedural errors 
of some significance; 
(c) Errors which may arise in the exercise of discretion which are plainly wrong, 
notwithstanding the latitude inherent in such exercise; and 
(d) Certain errors of fact. 
39. The Judge also quoted Deely v. Information Commissioner [2001] IEHC 91; [2001] 3 
I.R. 439 which involved an appeal on a point of law under the Freedom of Information Act 
1997, where the High Court (McKechnie J.) observed, at p. 452: - 
“There is no doubt but that where a court is considering only a point of law, whether 
by way of a restricted appeal or via a case stated, the distinction in my view being 
irrelevant, it is, in accordance with established principles, confined as to its remit, in 
the manner following: - 
(a) It cannot set aside findings of primary fact unless there is no evidence to 
support such findings; 

- 15 - 
(b) It ought not set aside inferences drawn from such facts unless such 
inferences were ones which no reasonable decision making body could draw; 
(c) It can however reverse such inferences, if the same were based on the 
interpretation of documents and should do if incorrect; and finally; 
(d) If the conclusion reached by such bodies shows that they have taken an 
erroneous view of the law, then that also is a ground for setting aside the 
resulting decision.” 
40. I note that in Sheedy v. Information Commissioner [2005] 2 I.R. 272, the Supreme 
Court broadly approved the principles in Deely, Fennelly J. observing that “the vehicle of 
appeal on a point of law cannot have been intended to involve the High Court, or, a 
fortiori, this court in detailed review of the respondent’s conclusions of fact”. 
41. In the present appeal, I do not understand the appellant as suggesting that the Judge 
erred in setting out the remit of the High Court in an appeal under s. 26(3)(b), rather, he 
asserts that the Judge wrongly applied the relevant principles to his case. I turn now to the 
Judge’s consideration of the issues before him. 
42. At the outset of the “Assessment and Decision” portion of the judgment, the Judge 
outlined his view that the appellant had not established that the judgment and order of the 
Circuit Court was wrong in holding that the decision of the DPC was not vitiated by 
serious and significant error or a series of such errors. Specifically, the Judge found that 
the appellant had not established that the decision of the Circuit Court involved a point of 
law pursuant to s. 26(3)(b) of the 1988 Act (para. 22). The bases for the Judge’s conclusion 
in this regard are found in the balance of the judgment and are addressed below. 
43. With regard to the five issues which the appellant claimed remained extant from his 
2010 complaint, by way of overview, the Judge considered that the first, third and fourth 
issues as raised in the appellant’s 12 May 2020 email related to the legality or legitimacy 

- 16 - 
of CAI’s regulations, measures, and marking schemes, rather than data protections issues 
and, so were outside of the DPC’s statutory remit (para. 26). He was of the view that the 
second issue comprised a complaint about the adequacy of CAI’s response to the initial 
subject matter request which request, in the opinion of the Judge, had to be viewed “in the 
context of what has occurred in the decade since that initial request”. I will return to the 
Judge’s observation in this regard when discussing the appellant’s complaint that the 
investigation undertaken by the DPC was a “mere assessment”. The Judge noted that the 
fifth issue dealt with a further separate matter, which was whether the appellant was 
entitled to the originals of the documentation furnished to him. 
44. After reprising the salient aspects of the DPC’s decision of 21 April 2022 (already 
set out above), and the decision reached by the Circuit Court, the Judge, at paras. 52-68 
went on to address the points of law asserted by the appellant in his notice of appeal to the 
High Court. 
45. Insofar as point 1 – the provision of data outside the statutory timeframe – was 
concerned, the Judge found that this did not form any part of the appellant’s reformulated 
complaints or the DPC’s decision of 21 April 2022. Whilst he noted that the appellant had 
referred to this point in his written submissions to the Circuit Court, “[i]t was not 
identified as a ground of appeal”. In those circumstances, the Judge considered that where 
it was never a part of the reformulated complaint or the decision in the first instance, point 
1 could not have formed part of the appeal to the Circuit Court and “cannot now be part of 
this appeal”. In any event, the Judge went on to note the point made by counsel for the 
DPC that as a matter of fact, the access request made on 12 May 2010 had been replied to 
within less than one month of that date by CAI on 1 June 2010, which was within the 40-
day timeframe set out in s. 4 of the 1988 Act. 

- 17 - 
46. The Judge next considered point 4 of the notice of appeal, viz, whether a “mere 
assessment” of a letter of complaint and supporting documentation by the DPC amounted 
to an investigation of the appellant’s complaint pursuant to s. 10 of the Data Protection 
Acts. 
47. The case the appellant advanced in the High Court on this ground was that the DPC 
did not carry out any investigation, which he said represented a serious error of law in 
circumstances, it was submitted, where the Supreme Court had directed that his 2010 
complaint be investigated afresh. Whilst there had been an “assessment” of his complaint 
by the DPC, there was no investigation or inspection. The appellant based his claim that 
there was no investigation in accordance with s. 10 of the 1988 Act by referring to the lack 
of recorded action by the DPC which would show any steps taken to deal with his 
complaint. Much of his complaint before the DPC, he said, related to his efforts to obtain 
information from CAI about his examination marks and documentation in relation to this, 
including original documentation, as he believed such documentation to be inaccurate. 
The appellant’s case in the High Court was that the DPC had dismissed his arguments and 
had based her decision in reliance on the decision of Haughton J. in Nowak v. The Data 
Protection Commissioner, a decision which the appellant contended related to different 
circumstances. 
48. As the Judge noted, the appellant was of the belief that the original documents were 
not accurate and that, in order to assess their accuracy, the DPC required access to this 
documentation. The appellant further contended that the arguments he had made before the 
Circuit Court had not been addressed in the judgment of the Circuit Court. 
49. The Judge (at para. 56) found that the complaint that there was an inadequate 
investigation was not borne out by the facts of the case, and that the appellant’s 
engagement with the DPC was reflected in the decision letter of 21 April 2022. He also 

- 18 - 
found the complaint of the absence of a formal notice of the investigation by the DPC and 
the alleged failure of the DPC to communicate with CAI to be without merit “having 
regard to the extensive engagement with the CAI and its legal advisors” and that the 
appellant’s argument “discounts the fact that this was a re-stated complaint arising from 
an initial 2010 complaint.” Accordingly, the Judge was satisfied that the assertion that 
there was an inadequate investigation “does not constitute a point of law pursuant to 
section 26(3)(b) …which constitutes an error, error of law or error of fact” which merited 
his overturning the decision of the Circuit Court. 
50. The Judge was also satisfied that the exercise by the DPC of her discretion “was 
entirely in accordance with her statutory mandate”, noting that in terms of the 
enforcement of data protection, s. 10(1)(a) of the 1988 Act provides that the Commissioner 
may investigate, or cause to be investigated, whether any of the provisions of the 1988 Act 
had been, are being or are likely to be contravened. He noted the provisions of s. 
10(1)(b)(i) and (ii) of the 1988 Act which provides that the Commissioner’s discretion is 
informed in the circumstances where a complaint is made to the Commissioner under 
s.10(1)(a) he/she is required to (“shall”), inter alia, investigate the complaint. Whilst the 
appellant had referred to certain provisions of the Data Protection Act 2018 (“the 2018 
Act”) (which were not applicable to this case), the Judge considered that “it is for the 
DPC, in the exercise of his or her statutory discretion (whether unamended or as it applies 
today) to determine the extent and scope of the investigation that it considers appropriate 
in any given case”. 
51. The Judge next considered points 2, 3 and 5 of the appellant’s notice of appeal all of 
which, in the view of the Judge, raised questions in relation to the DPC’s jurisdiction to 
rule on the legal status of CAI’s regulations and/or the legality of CAI’s internal rules or 
measures. He noted that these three points appeared to relate to the first of the five issues 

- 19 - 
set out in the appellant’s email of 12 May 2020. He was of the view that the attempt by the 
appellant to raise the legality or legitimacy of CAI’s own measures, which the DPC had 
reiterated were outside of her statutory remit, would involve the DPC “potentially acting 
ultra vires”, something which had been pointed out by the DPC to the appellant and which 
the appellant had indeed previously acknowledged. 
52. At para. 64, he said that, after an interval of a decade, the appellant had sought in 
2020 to incorporate new elements into a 2010 complaint and that the DPC was entitled to 
come to the view there was no evidence to suggest that CAI had failed to comply with the 
subject access request or that there was any outstanding documentation which remained to 
be given to the appellant. In those circumstances, he found no basis for overturning the 
decision of the Circuit Court in its consideration of those matters. Furthermore, assessing 
the decision of the Circuit Court “through the prism of Attorney General v. Davis and 
Deely v. Information Commissioner”, the Judge also had regard to the fact that the 
appellant had been offered the facility to inspect the documentation but had not availed of 
that facility. 
53. Insofar as the appellant was seeking his original examination scripts, relying on the 
decision of the CJEU in joined cases, C- 141/12, Y.S. v. Minister Voor Immigratie, 
Integratie en Asiel and C-372/12, Minister Voor Immigratie, Integratie en Asiel v M and S 
(ECLI: EU: C: 2014: 2081), [2015] 1 W.L.R. 609 and the decision of this Court in Nowak 
v. Data Protection Commission [2020] IECA 174 the Judge found that the appellant had no 
right to personal data in its original form. 
54. Hence, for the reasons he set out in the judgment, by order dated 8 October 2024 (as 
perfected on 1 November 2024) the Judge dismissed the appellant’s appeal against the 
judgment and order of the Circuit Court dated 9 October 2023. 
The Appeal 

- 20 - 
55. The appellant’s notice of appeal to this Court advances six grounds which are, in 
summary, that the Judge: 
• Erred in fact in holding that the appellant was employed by CAI (ground 3.1). 
• Failed to determine whether examination scripts could be regarded as personal 
data “in light of the unapproved Examination Regulations pursuant to Section 
6 of the Institute of Chartered Accountants in Ireland (Charter Amendment) 
Act 1966” (ground 3.2). 
• Erred in fact in holding that there was a reformulation of the 2010 complaint 
and that “the provision of data outside the statutory time-frame never formed 
part of the reformulated complaint and cannot now be part of this appeal” 
(ground 3.3). 
• Erred in law in holding that the appellant failed to identify serious and 
significant errors (ground 3.4). 
• Erred in law in holding that the appellant had sought to incorporate new 
elements into a 2010 complaint and in finding that there was no basis for 
overturning the decision of the Circuit Court (ground 3.5). 
• Erred in law in failing to determine whether a data subject is entitled to access 
the originals of his or her personal data in circumstances where there are 
suspicions of data erasure, unauthorised amendment or unlawful interference 
with such data (ground 3.6). 
Discussion and Decision 
56. From the notice of appeal and the parties’ submissions (written and oral), I consider 
that the following issues arise for consideration. Each of those issues (as identified 
hereunder) will be addressed in turn. 
(i)– The alleged error in holding that the appellant was employed by CAI 

- 21 - 
57. There is no dispute between the parties in respect of this ground. It is acknowledged 
by the DPC that the Judge erred in fact when he described the appellant as having been 
employed by CAI in circumstances where the appellant was simply undergoing a course of 
study facilitated by CAI and was not an employee. The DPC submits that the error is not 
material to the issues that fell to be decided by the Judge. I agree. As the DPC says, this 
was simply an error by the Judge in setting out the background to the proceedings. It had 
no bearing on the issues that fell to be considered by the Judge. More, particularly, as again 
the DPC points out, this ground of appeal discloses no error in the decision of 21 April 
2022 or in the decision of the Circuit Court which upheld the DPC’s decision. I also agree 
with the submission that the appellant could have sought to have this error corrected by 
making the appropriate application before the Judge prior to the perfecting of the High 
Court order. Moreover, this matter was not pursued by the appellant in his written or oral 
submissions to the Court. 
(ii) Alleged breach of s. 4(7) of the 1988 Act 
58. Section 4(7) of the 1988 Act provides: - 
“A notification of a refusal of a request made by an individual under and in 
compliance with the preceding provisions of this section shall be in writing and 
shall include a statement of the reasons for the refusal and an indication that the 
individual may complain to the commissioner about the refusal.” 
59. In his oral submissions to this Court, the appellant asserted that the Judge erred in 
failing to address and find that CAI breached the 1988 Act by failing to provide him with 
the reasons for its determination that his examination scripts did not constitute personal 
data and by reason of its failure to advise him that he could complain to the DPC about this 
issue, as provided for by s. 4(7) of the 1988 Act. 

- 22 - 
60. This issue was not addressed by the appellant in his notice of appeal to the High 
Court. The appellant, however, maintains that this issue was covered by ground 6 of his 
notice of appeal and that, in the course of the hearing in the court below, he made oral 
submissions in respect of same but that the issue was not determined by the Judge. The 
appellant asserts that ground 3.5 of his notice of appeal to this Court encompasses his 
complaint against the Judge in this regard. 
61. The DPC says that the s. 4(7) issue was not raised by the appellant before the DPC or 
before the Circuit Court or the High Court and was only first articulated at the hearing of 
the within appeal. It submits that the appellant could have raised this issue in May 2020 
when he was asked by the DPC to identify issues he claimed remained extant from his 
2010 complaint but did not do so. 
62. I am satisfied that the appellant cannot pursue this ground of appeal at this remove in 
circumstances where demonstrably the s. 4(7) issue never featured before the DPC, or in 
the appeal to the Circuit Court. Insofar as he contends that ground 6 of his notice of appeal 
to the High Court brought the matter to the attention of the Judge, it will be recalled that 
ground 6 merely asserts: “Further or other point of law advanced at the hearing of the 
appeal”. This is not a valid ground of appeal. The purpose of a notice of appeal is to 
identify the grounds on which the decision under appeal is challenged. It is not permissible 
to seek to introduce at the hearing of an appeal grounds or issues of which the respondent 
has had no prior notice. Even if ground 6 could be said to encompass the appellant’s 
grievance regarding s. 4(7) (which it does not), it remains the case that since the s. 4(7) 
issue did not feature before the DPC or the Circuit Court, the Judge could not be expected 
to embark on a consideration of something which never featured before the Circuit Court 
or the first instance decision maker. 

- 23 - 
63. I would also add, merely by way of comment, and not seeking to detract from the 
legal requirements imposed on a data controller by s. 4(7), that even if the matter had been 
brought to the attention of the DPC it would undoubtedly have observed that CAI’s letter 
of 1 June 2010 did in fact set out the reason (even if that reason was subsequently found to 
not be correct in law) why the examination scripts were not being furnished. It might also 
have adverted to the fact that, even if for the sake of argument, CAI had breached the 
provisions of s. 4(7), the appellant had nevertheless availed of his legal entitlement under s. 
10(1)(a) to complain to the DPC about CAI’s refusal to provide his examination scripts. 
(iii) The alleged failure by the CAI to comply with the timeframe provided for in s. 4(1) 
of the 1988 Act 
64. Section 4(1)(a) of the 1988 Act provides, in relevant part: - 
“(a) Subject to the provisions of this Act, an individual shall, if he or she so 
requests a data controller by notice in writing— 
(i) be informed by the data controller whether the data processed by or on behalf of 
the data controller include personal data relating to the individual, 
(ii) if it does, be supplied by the data controller with a description of— 
(I) the categories of data being processed by or on behalf of the data 
controller, 
(II) the personal data constituting the data of which that individual is the 
data subject, 
(III) the purpose or purposes of the processing, and 
(IV) the recipients or categories of recipients to whom the data are or may 
be disclosed, 
(iii) have communicated to him or her in intelligible form— 

- 24 - 
(I) the information constituting any personal data of which that individual is 
the data subject, and 
(II) any information known or available to the data controller as to the 
source of those data unless the communication of that information is 
contrary to the public interest, 
and 
(iv) where the processing by automatic means of the data of which the individual is 
the data subject has constituted or is likely to constitute the sole basis for any 
decision significantly affecting him or her, be informed free of charge by the data 
controller of the logic involved in the processing, 
as soon as may be and in any event not more than 40 days after compliance by the 
individual with the provisions of this section and, where any of the information is 
expressed in terms that are not intelligible to the average person without 
explanation, the information shall be accompanied by an explanation of those 
terms.” 
65. At ground 3.3 of his notice of appeal to this Court, the appellant asserts, inter alia, 
that the Judge erred in holding that the provision of data outside the statutory timeframe 
provided for in the 1988 Act never formed part of the reformulated complaint and could 
not therefore be part of the appeal to the High Court. 
66. This ground of appeal encompasses point 1 of the appellant’s appeal to the High 
Court on a point of law. It was addressed by the Judge at paras. 52 – 53 of the judgment. 
As we have seen, whilst the Judge acknowledged that there was reference to the issue in 
the appellant’s written submissions to the Circuit Court, he found that the issue did not 
form any part of the appellant’s reformulated complaints in May 2020, or any part of the 
DPC’s decision of 21 April 2022 or his appeal to the Circuit Court. Accordingly, in the 

- 25 - 
view of the Judge, it could not properly be the subject of an appeal to the Circuit Court or 
constitute an appeal to the High Court on a point of law. In essence, the Judge considered 
that the point had not been identified as a ground of appeal. 
67. The Judge was correct to find as he did. In circumstances where the alleged failure of 
CAI to respond to the appellant’s data request within forty days was not raised by the 
appellant in his email of 5 May 2020 and, consequently, never formed part of the DPC’s 
decision or the appeal to the Circuit Court, I cannot see how the Judge erred in addressing 
the issue as he did at para. 52 of the judgment. Moreover, as the DPC points out, the 
appellant was very clearly asked on 7 May 2020 to identify the outstanding issues from his 
2010 complaint, which he did without identifying any complaint concerning non-
compliance with s. 4(1) of the 1988 Act. I agree with the DPC that it is not permissible for 
the appellant to introduce new grounds of appeal within the narrow confines of an appeal 
on a point of law: the DPC’s decision of 21 April 2022 having addressed the five 
complaints the appellant actually identified and the Circuit Court in turn having considered 
the appellant’s appeal against the DPC’s findings in respect of those five issues. 
68. In any event, as we see from para. 53 of the judgment, the Judge went on to reprise 
the DPC’s submission that the appellant’s data request of 12 May 2010 was in fact replied 
to by CAI on 1 June 2010, which was within less than a month of the request. In other 
words, CAI responded to the subject access request within the 40-day time limit. 
69. Whilst the appellant’s grievance relates to the furnishing by CAI of his examination 
scripts only in 2018, some seventeen documents were furnished to the appellant on 1 June 
2010, well within the statutory timeframe. As is well rehearsed by now, in June 2010 CAI 
was of the view, based on legal advice, that the examination scripts did not constitute the 
appellant’s personal data, and that view was the subject of ongoing litigation. That issue 
was resolved by the CJEU’s ruling in 2017 consequent upon which the Supreme Court 

- 26 - 
remitted the complaint to the DPC in March 2018. It is common case that CAI provided 
the appellant with copies of his examination scripts on 18 June 2018. 
70. In my view, the circumstances in which the appellant only received his examination 
scripts in June 2018 do not undermine the fact that he received a response from CAI to his 
subject access request on 1 June 2010, which was well within the 40-day timeframe 
provided for in the 2018 Act. In any event, as the DPC and the Judge point out, had he 
wished to raise any issue in respect of compliance with the statutory timeframe as part of 
his 2010 complaint, he was afforded the opportunity to do so on 7 May 2020, but in his 
response to that request he did not raise any issue regarding compliance with the statutory 
timeframe. 
(iv) The alleged error on the part of the High Court in failing to find that the DPC failed 
to investigate the appellant’s complaint and in holding that there was a reformulation in 
2020 of the appellant’s 2010 complaint. 
71. Paragraph 3.3 of the notice of appeal to this Court asserts that the Judge erred in 
fact in holding, effectively, that by the contents of his 12 May 2020 email the appellant had 
reformulated his original 2010 complaint. Notably, the claim that the DPC failed to 
investigate the appellant’s complaint is not specifically addressed in para. 3.3 or indeed 
elsewhere in the notice of appeal. The issue is, however, specifically addressed in his 
written and oral submissions to this Court and, to some extent, also incorporated in paras. 
3.4 and 3.5 of his notice of appeal, as indeed the DPC acknowledged in its written and 
submissions to the Court. 
72. It is fair to say that the argument that the Judge wrongly held that the 2010 complaint 
had been reformulated, and that he erred in failing to find that the DPC failed to investigate 
his complaint, constitute the nub of the appellant’s appeal to this Court. 

- 27 - 
73. In aid of his argument that the DPC had failed to investigate his complaint, the 
appellant points to the way the matter was dealt with following the Supreme Court’s 
remittal of the complaint to the DPC. He cites the fact that after the matter was remitted by 
the Supreme Court, no official letter was sent to him by the Office of the DPC advising 
that an investigation was being commenced. He says that in 2020, some two years after 
the Supreme Court order, he was asked by the solicitors for the DPC what he thought 
should be investigated, an approach, he contends, which was contrary to the provisions of 
the 1988 Act. Whilst he had responded as he had on 12 May 2020 to the request made by 
the DPC’s solicitors to set out what remained outstanding from his 2010 complaint, he 
submits that contrary to the views expressed by the DPC and the Judge, in responding as 
he did, he had not reformulated his 2010 complaint. He says that the fact that he had 
responded to the DPC’s request did not relieve the Commissioner of her obligation to 
investigate his 2010 complaint. 
74. The appellant maintains that the DPC was obliged to investigate his 2010 complaint 
ab initio and that he had only set out the five items that appear in his 12 May 2020 email in 
response to the DPC’s request that he do so. 
75. He asserts that the High Court’s (and the Circuit Court’s) findings that his complaint 
was investigated by the DPC were arrived at in circumstances where in fact no 
investigation pursuant to s. 10(1) of the 1988 Act took place between 2020 and 2022. He 
points to the lack of any extensive engagement by the DPC with CAI during this period. 
As the appellant puts it in his written submissions “none was requested and none was 
received by the Commission in order to independently establish facts in order to apply law 
later on”. The appellant’s contention is that “[t]he Commissioner’s investigation must be 
based on principles of fundamental and procedural fairness. This condition was not met”. 

- 28 - 
76. In essence, the appellant asserts that what the DPC did in the period 2020-2022 was 
not the investigation prescribed by the 2010 Act, and that what the DPC in fact engaged in 
was a “mere assessment” of his complaint. 
77. Whilst he acknowledges that reference is made by the DPC in her decision of 21 
April 2022 to correspondence which passed between CAI and the appellant on 18 June 
2018 and 1 July 2018 in which the appellant was furnished by CAI with copies of his 
examination scripts and afforded the opportunity to inspect the originals of same, the 
appellant contends that he does not know how the DPC obtained access to this 
correspondence. Although not part of his pleadings or addressed in his written 
submissions to the Circuit Court, the High Court or this Court, the appellant contends that 
the fact that such correspondence found its way to the DPC suggests bias on the part of the 
DPC. 
78. It is far too late in the day for the appellant to allege bias on the part of the DPC, 
particularly in the absence of a shred of evidence to sustain such a serious allegation. 
79. It is the case that that, after the Supreme Court remitted the complaint, the DPC did 
not issue a formal notice of the investigation to either the appellant or CAI. Counsel for the 
DPC submits that nothing turns on this and points to the fact that s. 10 of the 1988 Act says 
nothing about the nature or form of the investigation that is to be conducted by the DPC. 
Insofar as the appellant relies on the 2018 Act as to the steps the DPC should have 
undertaken, the DPC’s position is that the 2018 Act was not applicable to the complaint, as 
indeed the appellant himself acknowledged. 
80. In any event, the DPC maintains that it did open an investigation after the Supreme 
Court remittal of the complaint. It says this is evidenced by the contents of an email sent to 
the appellant on 7 May 2020 by the solicitor for the DPC. The said email reads, in relevant 
part: - 

- 29 - 
“You’ll recall that after the Court of Justice and Supreme Court rulings in 2017, 
2018, the DPC opened an investigation, as directed by the Supreme Court. 
During that investigation, [CAI] agreed to give you copies of your exam scripts 
(and related materials). The DPC understands that [the CAI’s] confirmation that 
they would release the script etc to you had the effect of dealing with the substance 
of the complaint, so that there was nothing else left for the DPC to make a decision 
on. (Obviously there was still an issue between us as to whether or not you were 
entitled to the original script etc. but that’s a different point).” 
81. Counsel for the DPC says that the investigation resolved quickly in circumstances 
where by June 2018 CAI had complied with the appellant’s request, provided him with 
copies of his examination scripts and offered him the opportunity to inspect the originals. 
Counsel submits that the DPC’s approach must also be viewed in the context where the 
balance of the appellant’s personal data had long before been furnished to him on 1 June 
2010. 
82. It is in those circumstances, together with the view adopted by the DPC as to the 
nature of the issues raised by the appellant on 12 May 2020, that the DPC contends that it 
was not necessary for it to correspond further with CAI in relation to the appellant’s 
complaint. It points to the fact that all of the matters raised by the appellant in his 12 May 
2020 email were duly addressed in the 21 April 2022 decision. Accordingly, the DPC 
denies any breach of fair procedures on her part. 
83. I consider that whilst it might have been better if some formal communication had 
been sent by the DPC to the appellant in 2018 after the 2010 complaint was remitted by the 
Supreme Court, the fact of the matter is that very shortly thereafter, CAI engaged in 
correspondence with the appellant. As we see, under cover of letter dated 18 June 2018, 
CAI’s solicitors agreed to furnish (and did in fact furnish) him with copies of his 

- 30 - 
examination scripts. On 11 July 2018, it offered to facilitate an inspection of the original 
scripts. It bears repeating that this was done against a backdrop whereby in response to his 
May 2010 subject access request, the appellant had been furnished with copies of some 
seventeen documents on 1 June 2010 which were said by CAI at the time to constitute his 
personal data. As we know, in June 2010, CAI were of the view that the appellant’s 
examination scrips did not constitute his personal data. 
84. That position of course changed after the CJEU’s ruling. However, prior to the 
CJEU’s ruling, the fact of the matter is that (insofar as the procurement by the appellant of 
his of personal data is concerned) the only issue outstanding from 2010 were the 
appellant’s examination scripts, which had to await the outcome of the appellate process in 
which the appellant engaged after the Circuit Court upheld the DPC’s decision to decline 
to investigate the 2010 complaint, part of which process included the reference made by 
the Supreme Court to the CJEU. There is nothing to suggest, in that timeframe, that the 
appellant was maintaining that there was other data with which he should have been 
furnished. To my mind, therefore, once the appellant was in fact furnished with copies of 
his examination scripts in June 2018, and in circumstances where CAI had furnished him 
with some seventeen documents on 1 June 2010, it is not unsurprising that there was little 
procedural engagement by the DPC with the appellant in the period June 2018 to May 
2020. 
85. As already referred to, it is the case that on 5 May 2020, in the course of a hearing 
before this Court in relation to the appellant’s appeal in relation to his 2014 complaint, it 
was brought to the attention of the DPC that the appellant considered that a number of 
matters remained outstanding arising from his 2010 complaint. Pursuant to a request made 
by the solicitor for the DPC on 7 May 2020, these matters were duly set out in the 
appellant’s 12 May 2020 email and duly addressed in the decision of 21 April 2022. 

- 31 - 
86. I turn now to the question whether, as the appellant contends, the High Court should 
have found procedural and/or substantive frailties in that decision, such as would warrant 
the setting aside by this Court of the respective orders of the High Court and the Circuit 
Court, bearing in mind that the appellant’s right of appeal to the High Court was solely an 
appeal on a point of law from the decision of the Circuit Court. 
87. The decision of 21 April 2022 addressed the five issues raised by the appellant in his 
12 May 2022 email. The first issue concerned the legality of the measures taken by CAI as 
regards the examinations undertaken by the appellant. What appears to have been behind 
this was a suggestion that the 2009 examinations had been conducted under bye-laws - or 
purported bye-laws - which had not been submitted to and allowed by the Government, as 
required by s. 6 of the 1966 Act. As found by the DPC, this fell outside its remit. In those 
circumstances, the DPC did not require CAI to be consulted on this issue. 
88. The Judge identified points 2, 3 and 5 of the appellant’s notice of appeal as 
questioning the legality of CAI’s internal rules and regulations. These points essentially 
reprised the first of the five issues set out in the appellant’s 12 May 2020 email. As the 
Judge held, the appellant’s attempt to raise the legality or legitimacy of CAI’s own 
measures would involve the DPC potentially acting ultra vires. Moreover, as the Judge 
(and the DPC) observed, this first issue comprised a new point which had not been raised 
by the appellant in his 2010 complaint which, of itself, would justify the rejection of this 
complaint. In any event, the DPC went on to consider the substance of the complaint but 
found it to be outside of its remit. 
89. I am satisfied that the Judge was entirely correct to uphold the DPC’s decision that it 
had no authority or jurisdiction to make any findings in relation to any alleged failure on 
the part of CAI to comply with its obligations under the 1966 Act. None of the arguments 

- 32 - 
the appellant makes at paras. 14-21 or 33-35 of his written submissions persuade me 
otherwise. 
90. This also holds true for the third issue raised by the appellant in his May 2020 email, 
and I am satisfied the Judge (and the DPC) were again correct to find that issues sought to 
be canvassed in relation to the legitimacy of CAI’s marking scheme and the reports of the 
examiner and moderator did not fall within the DPC’s statutory remit. 
91. In determining the above issues, both the DPC and the High Court had the benefit of 
the decision of this Court in Nowak v. the Data Protection Commissioner [2020] IECA 174 
where Haughton J. expressed the view, at para. 48, that questions as to the lawfulness of 
the regulations pursuant to which CAI conducted the relevant examinations “would seem 
to have no relevance to an appeal under section 26 of the Data Protection Acts 1988-
2003”. I concur with that view. Had the appellant wished to challenge CAI’s conduct and 
oversight of the relevant examination process, his remedy lay elsewhere. 
92. It will be recalled that via the second issue raised in his 12 May 2020 email, the 
appellant contended that CAI failed to comply with his 2010 request in relation to three 
sub-categories of documents each of which, the appellant contended, contained personal 
data relating to him. In considering this issue, in its decision letter of 21 April 2022 the 
DPC noted that CAI’s letter of 1 June 2010 (which enclosed copies of seventeen 
documents) included a copy letter dated 4 March 2010 “confirming the outcome of [the 
appellant’s] appeal” and a “copy report submitted by Examiner & Moderator on remark 
of [the appellant’s] Autumn script”. The DPC also noted that, separately, under cover of 
the letter of 18 June 2018 from CAI’s solicitors, the appellant was provided with a “Copy 
of both reports on appeals re. Marking of the autumn Script 330316”. (That letter also 
enclosed the marking plan for script 330676 -the summer examination- and the marking 
plan for script 330316-the autumn script). 

- 33 - 
93. The decision letter of 21 April 2022 went on to state that it was the Commissioner’s 
understanding that the documents released to the appellant on 1 June 2010 and 18 June 
2018 comprised the full extent of the appellant’s personal data arising from the appeal 
panel’s consideration of his appeal. It was also said that the Commissioner was not in 
receipt of any information which on an objective and fair assessment could be said to 
support the contention that the appellant had identified other personal data that should have 
been released to him. In essence, the DPC’s position was that it was not for it to 
interrogate on how the examination appeal panel considered the appellant’s appeal, or to 
seek to look behind documents which on their face indicated his appeal had been 
considered. Nor, it was said, was it the Commissioner’s role to assess whether CAI had 
complied with its own internal rules in connection with the conduct of appeals. 
94. It will be recalled that the DPC also observed, without prejudice to the views it 
expressed as to its remit, that it was not at all clear that the marking scheme (which was 
applicable to all candidates) could sensibly be said to contain personal data. 
95. In this appeal, the DPC’s position is that the appellant has not, either before the High 
Court or this Court, pointed to any outstanding personal data being held by CAI which he 
has not received. As Ms. Skehan avers at para. 33 of her replying affidavit: - 
“In the Grounding Affidavit, the Appellant has failed to identify any factual basis in 
support of his contention that the CAI did not comply with the subject access 
request in respect of these specific sub-categories of documents”. 
96. Counsel for the DPC also points to the fact that in a further affidavit sworn by the 
appellant on 11 January 2023, all the appellant says in relation to the second issue raised in 
his 12 May 2020 email is that “the evidence was provided to the Commissioner and that ‘it 
required however little logical thinking and analysis of documents provided under the 
letter of complaint. The officer of the Commission failed to do so”. 

- 34 - 
97. In the absence of the appellant pointing to some concrete evidence or information 
having been before the DPC which suggested that CAI was withholding other documents 
that contained the appellant’s personal data, I concur with the DPC’s argument that his 
averments in this regard are unclear and non-specific. Absent any clarity from the 
appellant, it is not apparent that there are outstanding documents being held by CAI. 
98. That this is the position is, in my view, supported by the fact that from 1 June 2010 
(when the appellant was first furnished with documentation by CAI), the sole issue of 
contention (as far as obtaining his personal data is concerned) was that his examination 
scripts had not been provided. As I have already observed, once copies of those 
documents were ultimately provided in June 2018, the appellant did not thereafter point to 
anything from which it could reasonably and objectively be deduced that CAI was still in 
possession of personal data pertaining to the appellant that had not been handed over. 
99. In all of those circumstances, the Judge correctly determined that the DPC was 
entitled to come to the view that there was no evidence to suggest that there was 
outstanding documentation and that there was no basis to overturn the Circuit Court’s 
consideration of those matters. For all the reasons set out above, I am satisfied that the 
investigation conducted by the DPC was entirely adequate and well within the discretion 
afforded her under the Data Protection Acts as to the scope or extent of any particular 
examination required to be conducted by the DPC pursuant to s. 10(1) of the 1988 Act. 
100. As we see from the notice of appeal and the appellant’s submissions, one of the 
matters with which he took issue was that the Judge had wrongly characterised his 
complaint as “reformulated”, and he submits that the Judge erred in law in this regard. For 
the avoidance of doubt, I find no such error on the part of the Judge. In truth, the 
appellant’s complaint was in fact reformulated by the inclusion of the first issue set out in 
his 12 May 2020 email which had not been canvassed in his original complaint. 

- 35 - 
Furthermore, in my view, whatever matters the appellant wished to articulate in 2020, they 
were being articulated against a landscape which had changed substantially since he made 
his original complaint in May 2010. By June 2018, he was in receipt of copies of his 
examination scripts. Hence, whatever way one looks at it, his original complaint that he 
was entitled to his examination scripts was satisfied (whatever about the other issues such 
as access to the originals of the scripts he was complaining about) such that that factor, of 
itself, meant that the matters which the DPC addressed in its 21 April 2022 decision could 
never be the same complaint as that first presented in May 2010. 

(v) The request for the originals of the appellant’s personal data 
101. The fifth issue raised by the appellant in his 12 May 2020 email was whether CAI 
was correct to refuse him “access to the originals” of his personal data, to include the 
marking schemes. There was some confusion -on both sides- as to what this was all about. 
The objective fact of the matter is that the appellant was offered the opportunity to inspect 
his original examination scripts. That being so, the appellant’s argument could only be 
understood as being -as it was understood by the DPC to be- that he was entitled to be 
given the original scripts. The position advanced by the DPC in the court below in respect 
of this issue was that in Nowak v. the Data Protection Commissioner [2020] IECA 174 this 
Court had concluded that the appellant was not entitled to access the original of any 
examination scripts or related documentation containing his personal data. Having 
considered the case law of the CJEU on this issue, and the judgment of Haughton J. in 
Nowak v. Data Protection Commission [2020] IECA 174, the Judge came to the same 
conclusion. 
102. In his written submissions, the appellant asserts as follows: - 

- 36 - 
“In light of the allegations of unlawful amendments to documents and the quality of 
copies provided in response to the [data access request], a data subject has a right 
to obtain access to the originals. If access to the originals is denied, then it is a 
matter for the Commissioner to decide whether such access is warranted following 
a proper investigation. As submitted above, there was no proper investigation 
undertaken.” 
He submits that the Data Protection Acts do not disallow access to the originals and that “it 
is logical when suspicions of irregularities on face of the copies provided in response to 
the data access request a data subject must have a right of access to the originals to check 
the copies against the originals especially in circumstances where the Act does not require 
a data controller or data process to provide certified copies of personal data.” 
103. Leaving aside the absence of any basis for any suspicion of irregularity, the fact is 
that the appellant was afforded the opportunity to check the copies against the originals but 
had not availed of that opportunity. 
104. Refuting any entitlement on the part of the appellant to access original 
documentation, the DPC points to the provisions of s. 4 of the 1988 Act which provides 
only that an individual is entitled to: 
“Have communicated to him or her in intelligible form- 
(i) The information constituting any personal data of which that individual is the 
data subject, and 
(ii) Any information known or available to the Data Controller as to the source of 
those data unless the communication of that information is contrary to the 
public interest …” 

- 37 - 
105. The DPC also submits that there is no automatic right to specific documents, a 
position which has been confirmed by the CJEU, and by this Court in Nowak v. The Data 
Protection Commissioner [2020] IECA 174. 
106. As is clear from Haughton J.’s judgment in the latter case, the appellant had argued 
in his written submissions in that case that “… access to the original of personal data 
would be specifically desirable if there are suspicions of manipulation, re-engineering of 
copies provided or fraud”. In his oral submissions in that case, he had argued that when a 
copy document is “doctored” then access to the original was desirable and that unless the 
data subject had access to the original, he/she could not “check to see whether the data is 
accurate”. The concerns communicated by the appellant included his belief that the copy 
he had received was not “accurate” and that he “wanted the originals because of the risk 
of manipulation … [they] can be doctored”. The appellant asserted “the right of the data 
subject to check for accuracy”. 
107. However, Haughton J. was “satisfied that the appellant cannot pursue the 
‘doctoring’ argument as it is based on hypothetical facts. The DPC and the Circuit Court 
on appeal, can only determine the complaint on the facts presented to them. The High 
Court on appeal, and now this Court on further appeal, under s. 26 can only determine a 
point of law. It must take the facts presented on affidavit and as found by the Circuit Court 
and determine the point of law raised on those facts.” He found that no evidence had been 
put before the DPC or the Circuit Court to ground a complaint of “manipulation”, “re-
engineering” or “doctoring”. 
108. In Nowak v. The Data Protection Commissioner [2020] IECA 174, the appellant had 
also relied on the last sentence of para. 58 of the judgment of the CJEU in Y.S. to support 
his contention that he had an entitlement to “the original file”. In this regard it is 
instructive to quote (as indeed Haughton J. did) paras. 57 -58 of the judgment in Y.S.: 

- 38 - 
“57. Although Directive 95/46 requires Member States to ensure that every data 
subject can obtain from the controller of personal data communication of all such 
data processed by the controller relating to the data subject, it leaves to the 
Member States to determine the actual material form that that communication must 
take, as long as it is ‘intelligible,’ in other words it allows the data subject to 
become aware of those data and to check that they are accurate and processed in 
compliance with that directive, so that that person may, where relevant, exercise 
the rights conferred on him by Articles 12(b) and (c), 14, 22 and 23 of the directive 
(see to that effect, the judgment in Rijkeboer, (EU: C: 2009: 293, paragraphs 51 
and 52). 
58. Therefore, insofar as the objective pursued by the right of access may be fully 
satisfied by another form of communication, the data subject cannot derive from 
either Article 12(a) of Directive 95/46 or Article 8(2) of the Charter the right to 
obtain a copy of the document or the original file in which those data appear. In 
order to avoid giving the data subject access to information other than the personal 
data relating to him, he may obtain a copy of the document or the original file in 
which that other information has been redacted”. 
109. Having regard to what was set out by the CJEU in Y.S., Haughton J. was of the view 
that the sentence relied on by the appellant required to be read “in the context of the earlier 
sentence where the CJEU was clearly stating that neither Article 12 of the Directive nor 
Article 8(2) of the Charter could support either the right to obtain a copy of the document, 
or the original file; it must also be read in the context of redaction from the original file of 
information other than personal data relating to the data subject. Moreover in Y.S. the 
CJEU was not called upon to consider or decide a claim for access to the original 
document containing personal data”. 

- 39 - 
110. In Nowak v. The Data Protection Commissioner [2020] IECA 174, the DPC relied on 
the judgment of the CJEU in Y.S. for the proposition that the data subject was only entitled 
under the Directive to a full summary of the data in an intelligible form sufficient to enable 
the data subject to exercise their rights under the Directive. In Y.S. the CJEU had been 
asked whether Article 12(2) could be interpreted to mean that there was a right to a copy of 
documents in which personal data had been processed. The CJEU answered that question 
in the following terms at para. 60 of its judgment, stating: 
“… Article 12(a) of Directive 95/46 and Article 8(2) of the Charter must be 
interpreted as meaning that an applicant for a residence permit has a right of 
access to all personal data concerning him which are processed by the national 
administrative authorities within the meaning of Article 2(b) of that directive. For 
that right to be complied with, it is sufficient for the applicant to be provided with a 
full summary of those data in an intelligible form, that is, a form which allows him 
to become aware of those data and to check that they are accurate and processed 
in compliance with that directive, so that he may, where relevant, exercise the 
rights conferred on him by that directive.” 
111. Having regard to the approach of the CJEU, Haughton J. was satisfied that “[f]ar 
from assisting the appellant, in my view the trial judge quite properly relied on the 
decision in Y.S. which clearly supports the argument of the DPC that under the Directive 
the data subject’s entitlement is to access to the relevant information/personal data in an 
‘intelligible form’, and does not support a right under the Directive to personal data in its 
original form”. 
112. The DPC had also relied on the decision of the Court of Appeal of England and 
Wales in joint appeals Ittihadieh v 5-11 Cheyne Gardens RTM Company Limited/Deer v. 
University of Oxford [2017] EWCA CIV 121, where the Court was considering s. 7(1)(c) 

- 40 - 
of the United Kingdom Data Protection Act, 1988, which is a counterpart of s. 4(1)(iii) of 
the 1988 Act. Having cited the decision of the CJEU in Y.S., Lewison L.J. went on to 
state, at para. 93: 
“93. The obligation under section 7(1)(c) includes an obligation to communicate in 
intelligible form the information constituting any personal data of which the 
individual is the data subject. This goes further than section 7(1)(b) which requires 
a description of the personal data. It is an obligation to supply the information 
itself. Even so, it is not an obligation to supply documents: Dunn v Durham County 
Council [2013] 1 WLR 2305, para 16. It is of critical importance to distinguish 
between the two. Although it may be more convenient and cheaper in some cases 
for a data controller to supply copy documents, there is no legal obligation to do 
so. It is very easy, however, to slip from dealing with personal data into dealing 
with electronically generated or stored documents in which personal data are 
recorded. It seems from many of the reported cases (as well as these two appeals) 
that individuals who make SARs [Subject Access Requests] are, in truth, looking for 
copy documents. They are in my judgment aiming at the wrong target. This ties in 
with the definition of personal data. Accepting as I do that a person’s name is his 
personal data, it does not follow that every piece of information in a document in 
which his name appears is his personal information. In such a case it would, in my 
judgment, be enough for the data controller to inform the data subject that, for 
instance, his name is consistently recorded as ‘Charles Pooter’ and his address as 
“The Laurels, Brickfield Terrace, Holloway” in a specified number of documents 
between particular dates. There would be no obligation to disclose the documents 
themselves. This is, I think, borne out by article 12 of the Directive which requires 
the data controller to inform the data subject of the categories of data concerned”. 

- 41 - 
113. Haughton J. was satisfied that the trial judge had correctly cited the judgment in 
Ittihadieh/Deer “as a persuasive authority supportive of the purposive approach adopted 
in Y.S. and the notion that the Directive is concerned with access to personal 
data/information and not with targeting documents per se”. (para. 62) 
114. It will be recalled that the fourth issue raised by the appellant in his May 2020 email, 
viz, “whether the CAI implemented all necessary and required by law security measures 
preventing unauthorised amendments to examination marks data”, was also held by the 
DPC (and the Judge) to fall outside the DPC’s statutory remit. I agree that that is so. 
115. At para. 39 of the judgment, the Judge noted that by raising this issue the appellant 
was seeking to question “again hypothetically” the legitimacy of the examination 
documentation, as he had done in his separate complaint and in respect of which, in Nowak 
v. Data Protection Commission [2020] IECA 174, Haughton J. accepted that it was not for 
the DPC to engage with or seek to determine entirely speculative allegations based on 
hypothetical facts in circumstances where no evidence of “doctoring” had been put 
forward by the appellant. 
116. In this appeal, the appellant seeks to revive the arguments he canvassed in Nowak v. 
The Data Protection Commissioner [2020] IECA 174 regarding the alleged manipulation 
or “doctoring” of his examination scripts and related material. At para. 10 of his written 
submissions, he contends that: - 
 “the following point of law…is essential in the context of the within appeal. 
a) Whether a data subject is entitled to access the originals of his/her personal
