data in circumstances where there are suspicions of data erasure, unauthorized 
amendment or unlawful interference with such data?” 
117. Save stating to this Court that he has “suspicions”, the appellant did not, in the 
course of the appeal hearing, point to any evidence of the alleged “data erasure” 

- 42 - 
“unauthorized amendment” or “unlawful interference”, or provide any reasonable basis 
upon which it could be concluded that such evidence might be forthcoming. Nor did he do 
so before the Commissioner even had the legitimacy of the examination process been 
something the Commissioner could have investigated. 
118. Furthermore, as observed by the Judge at para. 40, the Circuit Court in its judgment 
of 9 October 2023, at para. 11.1, also considered the appellant’s argument as being “at best 
a subjective analysis of hypothetical facts”, in the absence of any evidence having been put 
to that court. 
119. I also note that in his written submissions, in support of his argument that he should 
be provided with the originals of his examination scripts and related documents, he cites 
para. 68 of Nowak v. the Data Protection Commissioner [2020] IECA 174, where 
Haughton J. opined: 
“…It is conceivable that in certain circumstances a data controller may have an 
obligation to produce an original document containing personal data, for example, 
where it is required for rectification or erasure of data under section 6. Equally it 
may be that such document falls to be produced as a result of unlawful acts that 
give an entitlement to discovery or inspection for judicial review, plenary action or 
other legal proceedings, rather than under the Directive. Doubtless this is a 
question that will fall to be determined by another court on appropriate facts.” 
120. The appellant also refers to the Determination of the Supreme Court [2020] 
IESCDET 144 which refused him leave to appeal against the judgment of Haughton J., and 
he quotes paras. 11-13 thereof: - 
“11. The applicant was furnished with a copy of his script and has been offered the 
opportunity to inspect the original script, but has not chosen to take up this option 

- 43 - 
and he has not made out any argument that the copy might not be a true copy of the 
original. 
12. In the circumstances, the Court does not consider that the interests of justice 
justify the grant of leave, or that a matter of general public importance arises. 
13. The Court does not rule out the possibility that whether a data subject is 
entitled to an original document might raise a matter of general legal public 
importance or one that might justify leave in the interests of justice, such as when 
issues of data erasure or rectification arise. But the present application must be 
refused on account of its factual context where no legal basis has been made out 
that might justify a consideration of that point.” 
121. In my view, neither the circumstances envisaged by Haughton J. nor the scenario 
contemplated by the Supreme Court assist the appellant given that he has not pointed to 
any factor which might support his contention that his personal data was unlawfully 
interfered with or otherwise manipulated. 
122. Moreover, it bears repeating that in July 2018, the appellant was offered the 
opportunity to view the originals of his examination scripts. This was at a time when he 
had copies of those scripts which would have allowed him, at the very least, to test those 
copies against the originals. In my view, had he done so, he could have satisfied himself as 
to whether the “suspicions” he held were justified. He did not avail of that opportunity. I 
was not persuaded by explanation the appellant gave as to why he did not avail of the offer 
made by CAI. Whilst it may be the case that the offer was confined to the originals of his 
examination scripts and not the originals of the entirety of the documentation he received 
from CAI, that does not, to my mind, explain why the appellant did not avail of the offer. 
Summary 

- 44 - 
123. In my view, in this case, based on the facts before the Court, and in the absence of 
the appellant having established before the DPC that the position was otherwise, 
everything points to the appellant having been provided with “a faithful and intelligible 
reproduction” of his personal data, to borrow the phraseology of the CJEU in case C- 
487/21, FF v Österreichische Datenschutzbehörde, EU:C: 2023:369. For the reasons set 
out in this judgment, I would dismiss the appeal and affirm the Order of the High Court.
